title: Resources & Hints
text:
Resources
Search for relevant books, papers, proceedings, conferences etc. using:

CiteSeerX — scientific literature digital library
ACM Portal — ACM Digital Library
IEEE — IEEE Xplore Digital Library
Google Scholar — broad academic search engine
Wikipedia is NOT considered a trustworthy resource!!! (just for introduction to problematics)

title: Research Projects
text:
Research Areas
My research projects focus on:

Software Visualization

Visualizing software architecture in 3D environments
Understanding software dependencies and evolution
Tools for software comprehension and analysis



Graph Algorithms and Visualization

Developing efficient graph layout algorithms
Edge bundling techniques for complex networks
Interactive graph exploration tools



Augmented Reality & 3D Graphics

Photorealistic data visualization in AR
Real-time rendering techniques
Integration of virtual and physical environments



Visual Data Mining

Interactive data exploration interfaces
Pattern discovery in complex datasets
Visual analytics for large-scale data

title: Research Projects
text:
Collaboration Opportunities
I am interested in collaborating on projects involving: - Software engineering tools and visualization - Data visualization and visual analytics - 3D graphics and augmented reality applications - Graph algorithms and network visualization
For collaboration inquiries, please contact me.

title: Can quantum-mechanical description of physical reality be considered complete?
text:
Abstract
In a complete theory there is an element corresponding to each element of reality. A sufficient condition for the reality of a physical quantity is the possibility of predicting it with certainty, without disturbing the system. In quantum mechanics in the case of two physical quantities described by non-commuting operators, the knowledge of one precludes the knowledge of the other. Then either (1) the description of reality given by the wave function in quantum mechanics is not complete or (2) these two quantities cannot have simultaneous reality. Consideration of the problem of making predictions concerning a system on the basis of measurements made on another system that had previously interacted with it leads to the result that if (1) is false then (2) is also false. One is thus led to conclude that the description of reality as given by a wave function is not complete.

title: Can quantum-mechanical description of physical reality be considered complete?

title: Courses
text:
Graduate Study

Team Project
Graduate level course
Collaborative software development project where students work in teams to design, implement, and deliver a complex software system.

Focus Areas

Team collaboration and communication
Project management
Software development lifecycle
Code review and quality assurance
Documentation and presentation
Real-world problem solving




Student Work



Advanced Computer Graphics Methods
Graduate level course
The course focuses on providing theoretical background and understanding of advanced computer graphics techniques. Gain insight into the design and implementation of computer graphics applications that rely on the mastery of the GPU. The goal is to master the basic principles and algorithms behind rendering of photorealistic 3D scenes in real time.

Credits: 6
Assessment: Semestral tasks in courses (20b), Project (20b), Exam (60b)


Course Contents

Photorealistic rendering
Raytracing, Radiosity, Monte-Carlo
Procedural texturing
Shadow casting
Image based rendering
BRDF, models for lighting and materials
Volumetric rendering
Augmented reality and Virtual reality
Lightfield theory and stereoscopic rendering
Vertex/fragment shader programming




Student Work



Data Visualization
Graduate level course
The goal of this course is to familiarize students with the basic and advanced techniques of information visualization and scientific visualization, which are the basis of visual analytics. Students learn key techniques of the visualization process including data preparation, visual mapping, and visualization itself — useful for understanding large-scale abstract data, scientific data and processes.

Credits: 6
Assessment: Semester project (60%), mid-term test (10%), final written exam (30%)


Course Contents

Introduction to the topic
Visual perception, visual representation of data, Gestalt principles, information overload
Creating visual representations, visualization reference model, visual mapping, visual analytics
Design of visualization techniques, architecture of visualization systems, design patterns
Classification of visualization systems
Interaction and distortion techniques
Visualization of one-, two- and multi-dimensional data, text and text documents
Visualization of groups, trees, graphs, clusters, networks, software
Metaphorical visualization
Visualization of volumetric data, vector fields, processes and simulations
Visualization of maps, geographic information, GIS systems
Collaborative visualizations
Evaluating visualizations




Student Work

My research focuses on software visualization, interactive data visualization, visual data mining, and graph layout algorithms. I am particularly interested in 3D visualization techniques, augmented reality applications, and innovative approaches to understanding complex software systems.

Graph Layout & Visualization Algorithms
Interactive Data Visualization & Visual Data Mining
Augmented Reality Applications

title: Supervised Theses
text: Over the years, I have supervised more than 40 bachelor’s and master’s thesis projects across various topics in computer science, with a focus on visualization, graphics, and software engineering.

title: Supervised Theses
text: Over the years, I have supervised more than 40 bachelor’s and master’s thesis projects across various topics in computer science, with a focus on visualization, graphics, and software engineering.

title: Supervised Theses
text:
Interested?
Search in Yonban for my other projects - not all were assigned to students.  If the projects I am/was supervising are interesting and you would like to work on similar project, feel free to contact me.  However the process of defining new projects/thesis is a bit complicated, so for:


Bachelor project - contact me via mail in your 2nd undergraduate study year (mid of summer semester)


Master thesis - TO DO!



Research Areas
The supervised theses cover a wide range of topics including:

Software Visualization - 3D visualization of software architecture and dependencies
Graph Algorithms - Layout algorithms, clustering, and edge bundling techniques
Augmented Reality - AR applications for data and software visualization
Interactive Visualization - Web-based and desktop visualization tools
Data Mining - Visual data mining and analytics
Multimedia - Video processing, 3D graphics, and multimedia systems
Web Technologies - HTML5, WebGL, and interactive web applications

title: Supervised Theses
text:
Thesis Projects
Below is a list of supervised thesis projects. Use the filters to explore by topic or year.

title: Supervised Theses
text:
Current Opportunities
I am always looking for motivated students interested in: - Software visualization and visual analytics - 3D graphics and augmented reality - Graph algorithms and network visualization - Interactive data visualization - Web-based visualization tools
If you’re interested in working on a thesis project in these areas, please contact me to discuss potential topics.

What I Look For

Strong programming skills (Java, JavaScript, Python, C++)
Interest in visualization and graphics
Motivation to explore new technologies
Ability to work independently
Good communication skills

International Symposium on Intelligent Systems, June 1, 123–28. https://MarekPenzes.github.io/publications/visual-information-discovery-large-datasets/.

title: Visual programming environment based on hypergraph representations
text: In this paper we present a concept of a visual programming environment that uses hypergraphs for representing, querying and visualizing software artifacts. The hypergraph representation allows to store semantic relations between software artifacts and can be visualized with well-known graph drawing algorithms.

title: Research & Publications
text: The Vision & Graphics Group is an informal research group at the Institute of Applied Informatics at FIIT STU that is interested in computer vision, computer graphics, visualization and related problem fields.

title: Research & Publications
text: The Vision & Graphics Group is an informal research group at the Institute of Applied Informatics at FIIT STU that is interested in computer vision, computer graphics, visualization and related problem fields.

title: Research & Publications
text:
Research Areas
My research focuses on innovative approaches to understanding and visualizing complex software systems and data structures.

Primary Research Interests
Software Visualization in 3D Space Developing techniques for visualizing software architecture, dependencies, and evolution in three-dimensional environments to provide better understanding of complex systems.
Graph Layout & Visualization Algorithms Creating efficient algorithms for laying out and visualizing large graphs and networks, including edge bundling techniques to reduce visual clutter.
Interactive Data Visualization & Visual Data Mining Exploring interactive methods for data exploration and mining, enabling users to discover patterns and insights in complex datasets.
Augmented Reality Applications Investigating the use of augmented reality for photorealistic data visualization and software structure representation in real-world contexts.
Software Evolution & Metrics Visualization Analyzing and visualizing how software systems evolve over time, including metrics visualization and program execution dynamics.
Documentation Generation from Source Code Automated approaches to generate and visualize software documentation directly from source code analysis.

title: Research & Publications
text:
Research Projects
Details of current and past research projects will be listed in the Projects section.

title: Research & Publications
text:
Supervised Research

Thesis Supervision
I have supervised over 40 bachelor’s and master’s thesis projects (2007-2016+) in areas including:

Software visualization and 3D graphics
Graph layout algorithms
Augmented reality applications
Interactive data visualization
Software metrics and evolution analysis
Multimedia content visualization

Recent thesis topics include: - Photorealistic data visualization in augmented reality - Graph clustering and edge bundling algorithms - Program execution visualization - Dynamic object-oriented program animation - Interactive 3D browsing systems
For more details, see the Supervised Theses section.

title: Research & Publications
text:
Collaborations
I am actively seeking collaborations in: - Software engineering visualization - Data visualization and visual analytics - 3D graphics and augmented reality - Graph algorithms and network visualization
If you’re interested in collaboration or have research questions, please get in touch.

title: Principles of Computer Graphics and Image Processing
Principles of Computer Graphics and Image Processing
Courses
text:
#### Student Work

Data Visualization
Graduate level course
The goal of this course is to familiarize students with the basic and advanced techniques of information visualization and scientific visualization, which are the basis of visual analytics. Students learn key techniques of the visualization process including data preparation, visual mapping, and visualization itself — useful for understanding large-scale abstract data, scientific data and processes.

Credits: 6
Assessment: Semester project (60%), mid-term test (10%), final written exam (30%)


Course Contents

Introduction to the topic
Visual perception, visual representation of data, Gestalt principles, information overload
Creating visual representations, visualization reference model, visual mapping, visual analytics
Design of visualization techniques, architecture of visualization systems, design patterns
Classification of visualization systems
Interaction and distortion techniques
Visualization of one-, two- and multi-dimensional data, text and text documents
Visualization of groups, trees, graphs, clusters, networks, software
Metaphorical visualization
Visualization of volumetric data, vector fields, processes and simulations
Visualization of maps, geographic information, GIS systems
Collaborative visualizations
Evaluating visualizations
